title: Duplicate claimSummary construction logic
detail: The code that constructs claimSummary (filtering claims by status) is duplicated verbatim in runPlanningPath (lines 242-247) and runReviewDiscussionPath (lines 356-361). This violates DRY principle and creates maintenance risk.
fix: Extract claimSummary construction into a shared helper function: `function buildClaimSummary(allClaims: ClaimRecord[]): ClaimStatusSummary { ... }` and call it from both paths.

title: Path traversal vulnerability on Windows systems
detail: The validatePath function uses forward slash in the boundary check: `resolved.startsWith(REPO_ROOT + "/")`. On Windows, resolved paths use backslashes (\\), so this check fails and allows paths outside REPO_ROOT to pass validation. Relative paths like "..\\..\\secret.txt" could escape the boundary.
fix: Use path.sep instead of hardcoded '/': `resolved === REPO_ROOT || resolved.startsWith(REPO_ROOT + path.sep)`. Import path.sep from 'node:path'.

title: Unbounded line counting can cause memory bloat
detail: The execFileSafe function limits output to maxLines but splits the entire result string first (line 232: `raw.split("\n")`). For a 500KB grep result, this creates a full array in memory before slicing. If maxBuffer=512KB and the result is near that limit, splitting it can cause significant memory overhead.
fix: For large outputs, stream the result or count lines during split: use a custom split that counts lines up to maxLines without creating the full array, or use a more memory-efficient line-limiting approach.

title: Round-robin author rotation starts at index 1, not 0
detail: In pickNextAuthor(), round-robin policy uses `const idx = round % pool.length` where round starts at 1 in the main loop (line 94: for let r = 1). This means round 1 picks index 1 % N (index 1), round 2 picks index 2 % N, etc., skipping index 0 on the first rotation. If pool has [author, critic1, critic2], round 1 picks critic1, never author. Should be (round - 1) % pool.length or start round at 0.
fix: Change line 215 to `const idx = (round - 1) % pool.length;` to align with round starting at 1.

title: Entire revise prompt used as systemPrompt instead of instructions
detail: format.revisePrompt() returns a multi-paragraph string with task description, current draft content, critique details, and rules. This entire string is passed to createMessage as systemPrompt (line 28), when it should be split: the concise instructions as systemPrompt, and the context (subject, draft, critiques) as user messages. Passing huge context as systemPrompt wastes tokens and reduces clarity about what's system-level guidance vs. instance data.
fix: Refactor format.revisePrompt() to return {systemPrompt, userMessage} or adjust revise to build the message array separately with system vs user roles properly distinguished.

title: Missing abort signal in force-conclude message
detail: The force-conclude call to client.createMessage() on line 202 does not pass the `signal` parameter, unlike earlier calls on lines 97 and 90. If an AbortSignal was provided to runParticipantResearchWithDossiers, the force-conclude message will not respect cancellation requests.
fix: Add `signal,` parameter to the force-conclude client.createMessage call: `signal,` on line 202

title: Fragile error detection for tool results
detail: The evidence-keeping check on line 154 tests only `!result.startsWith('Error:')` but line 154 also mentions checking for 'Tool error:' and 'Unknown tool:' prefixes. However, the actual check only uses startsWith('Error:') and startsWith('Tool error:') and startsWith('Unknown tool:'). Also, this is brittle—if legitimate tool output starts with 'Error' as content, it would be incorrectly filtered.
fix: Create a helper function to detect errors more robustly: `const isToolError = (r: string) => r.startsWith('Error:') || r.startsWith('Tool error:') || r.startsWith('Unknown tool:');` and use that consistently

title: Relevant documents excluded due to discovery order
detail: The condition `if (isRelevant || found.size <= 5)` includes a document if it's relevant OR if fewer than 5 docs have been found. Once 5 irrelevant docs are added, highly relevant docs found later in iteration will be skipped. This causes relevant documents to be excluded based on discovery order rather than relevance.
fix: Change to include documents in two sequential phases: first collect all relevant docs, then fall back to first N docs if no relevant matches found. Or change line 55 to `if (isRelevant || (found.size < 5 && artifacts.length < 5))` to limit by artifacts added rather than found set size.

title: Truncate fails when no newline in first MAX_DOC_CHARS
detail: When content.length > MAX_DOC_CHARS, the function finds the last newline in the first MAX_DOC_CHARS chunk. If no newline exists (lastIndexOf returns -1), `t.slice(0, -1)` incorrectly removes the last character instead of using the full chunk. This creates malformed truncated output.
fix: Change to `return t.slice(0, lastNl >= 0 ? lastNl : t.length) + '\n... (truncated, ...'` to handle the no-newline case.

title: Truncate fails when no newline in first MAX_FILE_CHARS
detail: When content.length > MAX_FILE_CHARS, the function finds the last newline in the first MAX_FILE_CHARS chunk. If no newline exists (lastIndexOf returns -1), `t.slice(0, -1)` incorrectly removes the last character instead of using the full chunk. This creates malformed truncated output.
fix: Change to `return t.slice(0, lastNl >= 0 ? lastNl : t.length) + '\n... (truncated, ...'` to handle the no-newline case.

title: Unsafe null check on resolveLenses return value
detail: Line 40 assigns the result of resolveLenses(plan.lenses) to lenses, then line 41 immediately calls lenses.length without null/undefined checks. If resolveLenses can return null or undefined instead of an array, this will throw 'Cannot read property length of null/undefined'.
fix: Add null check before line 41: `if (!lenses || lenses.length === 0) return strategy;` or ensure resolveLenses always returns an array (even empty)

title: extractJSONArray uses greedy regex that captures multiple arrays as one
detail: Line 79: The regex `/\[\s\S]*\]/` is greedy and matches from the FIRST `[` to the LAST `]` in the text. If LLM response contains multiple arrays or object with nested arrays (e.g., `{reviews: [...], metadata: [...]}`), this will capture the entire string from first bracket to last, not just the intended array. This causes JSON.parse to fail or parse wrong structure.
fix: Use non-greedy matching: `/\[[\s\S]*?\]/` or better yet, try to find balanced bracket pairs like the brace logic in extractJSON (lines 49-68).

title: Race condition between descriptor lookup and settings update
detail: setEnabled() calls this.list() at line 79 to find a descriptor, which internally reads settings via settings.get(). Then at line 82, it calls settings.get() again to read the current state before writing. Between these two independent reads, another process/thread could modify the settings. For mcpServers with record-flag mode (line 87), this means the token found in the descriptor may no longer exist in the freshly-read mcpServers, causing a silent failure return at line 87 rather than throwing an error or applying the update.
fix: Call settings.get() once before passing to both list() and the update logic, or redesign to find the descriptor from the current settings state directly without a separate list() call.

title: AgentServer instance not retained, risking garbage collection
detail: The AgentServer is instantiated but the result is not assigned to any variable (line 165: `new AgentServer(...)`). While the constructor sets up a handler closure that captures `this`, the instance itself is not explicitly retained in module scope. If garbage collection occurs before the handlers are invoked, the AgentServer instance could be collected, potentially losing state (pendingApprovals map, approvalTimers, abortController, bgAgentBusUnsubscribe). The closures in StdioTransport's handlers array should theoretically keep it alive, but this is an implicit, fragile dependency on closure capture semantics.
fix: Assign the AgentServer instance to a module-level variable: `const agentServer = new AgentServer({...})` to ensure explicit retention and clarity that the instance is intentionally long-lived.

title: Underestimated replacement size in applyToolResultBudget
detail:
At line 349, the code assumes truncated tool results will be replaced with ~200 chars: `remaining += 200`. However, the actual replacement message (lines 360-363) includes `Output too large (${sizeKb}KB) — truncated to fit the per-message budget. Re-run the originating tool if you need the full output.

Preview (first 500 chars):
${preview}`, which is approximately 150 + 500 = 650+ chars. This causes the `remaining` size estimate to be wildly inaccurate (off by 450+ chars), leading to under-truncation that could exceed the maxTotalChars budget.
fix: Update line 349 to: `remaining += 656;` or better yet, calculate the actual replacement string length dynamically using the preview and sizeKb values

title: Silent default fallback masks invalid schedule configuration
detail: parseSchedule returns a hardcoded 10-minute interval (line 136) for any schedule string that doesn't match the expected formats or a valid millisecond number. This silently masks misconfiguration (e.g., typos like '5mn' instead of '5m') without logging or raising an error, making it hard to debug incorrect scheduling.
fix: Throw an Error or log a warning when schedule parsing falls back to the default, or validate the schedule string format strictly and reject invalid inputs.

title: Unsafe error message access on caught exception
detail: Line 73 casts the caught error to Error unconditionally: `(err as Error).message`. In JavaScript, non-Error objects can be thrown (e.g., strings, null, objects). If a non-Error value is thrown, the `.message` property will be undefined, resulting in `error: undefined` in the returned SyncResult. The error field is optional but should contain a meaningful string when an error occurs.
fix: Use `err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err)` to safely extract the error message, ensuring a string is always returned for the error field.

title: restore() method signature allows async implementations but callers don't await
detail: The CostStateStore.restore() method is declared as synchronous (returns void, not Promise<void>). However, at engine.ts line 946-948, the call is not awaited, and in an async context. If an implementation needs to perform async work (database restore, file I/O), it cannot return a Promise because TypeScript will reject it as not matching the void return type. This creates a type contract that prevents async implementations despite the async context where restore() is called.
fix: Change signature to 'async restore(state: CostStateSnapshot): Promise<void>' to explicitly allow async implementations, and update all call sites (engine.ts lines 947, 1373-1377, 1438-1439) to await the call.

title: restore() method lacks error handling contract for invalid input
detail: The restore() method accepts CostStateSnapshot (unknown type) with no validation requirements specified in the interface. If serialized state on disk is corrupted or malformed, implementations have no guidance on error handling—they may silently fail, throw, or crash. At engine.ts line 946-948, restore() is called unconditionally if costState exists, with no try/catch. A corrupt state could silently break cost tracking or cause an unhandled exception.
fix: Document the contract: either (1) require implementations to validate input and throw FrameworkError on invalid data, with engine.ts wrapping the call in try/catch, or (2) change signature to 'restore(state: CostStateSnapshot): boolean' to allow graceful failure signaling.

title: Double Map.get() call on same key
detail: The code calls `this.compactedMessagesBySession.get(options.sessionId)` twice — once in the condition check (line 929) and once to retrieve the value (line 930). This is inefficient and the standard anti-pattern. Should cache the result of the first .get() call.
fix: Store the result of .get() in a variable and reuse it: `const cached = this.compactedMessagesBySession.get(options.sessionId); messages = cached ? [...cached] : session.transcript.toMessages();`

title: Incorrect logger.warn() call signature
detail: logger.warn is called with a single string argument (`logger.warn("persistActiveModel failed: ...")`) but all other logger.warn calls in this file use the correct signature with an event key and an object: `logger.warn("event_name", { details })`. This call on line 1811 passes only a string, which doesn't match the logging framework's API.
fix: Change to: `logger.warn("engine.persist_active_model_failed", { error: (err as Error).message });`

title: Aspect ratio fallback assumes square image on bitmap read failure
detail: Lines 159-160 use `t.dim` as fallback for both width and height if `pic.bitmap` is undefined. This assumes a square image, which is incorrect for rectangular images. If Jimp fails to properly initialize bitmap dimensions, the aspect ratio calculations at lines 164-168 would produce distorted images. While Jimp should always set bitmap after a successful read, the fallback logic silently masks this potential issue instead of failing fast.
fix: Either validate that bitmap exists and has valid dimensions after Jimp.read(), or throw an error rather than assume square dimensions. Consider: `if (!pic.bitmap?.width || !pic.bitmap?.height) throw new Error('...')`

title: Unchecked array access with mismatched toolCalls and results lengths
detail: The loop at line 32 iterates over toolCalls.length using index i, but accesses results[i] at line 34 without validating that results.length >= toolCalls.length. If results array is shorter than toolCalls (due to upstream bugs or edge cases), accessing results[i] returns undefined, and calling .result or .error on undefined at line 36 throws.
fix: Add validation at the start: if (toolCalls.length !== results.length) return null; This ensures the arrays match before processing.

title: Synchronous manage() call outside try/catch block, inconsistent with async pattern
detail: Line 658 calls messages = this.deps.contextManager.manage(messages) outside the try block that wraps the main loop (lines 242-649). This is inconsistent with line 290 which calls await this.deps.contextManager.manageAsync(messages) inside the try block. If manage() can throw exceptions, they will not be caught by the error handler at line 635, leaving the session in an inconsistent state.
fix: Either (1) move line 658 inside the try block, or (2) wrap it in a separate try/catch block to ensure exceptions are handled consistently, or (3) explicitly clarify that manage() is synchronous and cannot throw.

title: Unvalidated split result in getGitLog may cause undefined property access
detail: At line 84, the code destructures `line.split("|")` into four variables without checking that the split produces exactly 4 elements. If a commit message contains a pipe character or the git format is malformed, accessing `hash`, `message`, `author`, or `date` could be undefined, causing crashes or silent data loss.
fix: Add validation: `const parts = line.split("|"); if (parts.length < 4) continue;` or use `const [hash = "", message = "", author = "", date = ""] = line.split("|");`

title: Hardcoded HEAD hash truncation assumes 8-character format
detail: At line 134, `line.slice(5, 13)` extracts exactly 8 characters for the HEAD hash. Git's porcelain format for worktree does not guarantee a fixed-width field; if git versions differ or the format changes, this will truncate or misalign the hash. The format is 'HEAD <hash>' but the length of the hash is not fixed.
fix: Replace `line.slice(5, 13)` with `line.slice(5).split(/\s/)[0]` or similar to extract the full hash token, not a fixed slice.

title: Zero context window overwrites valid static catalog data
detail: In enrichFromStaticCatalog (line 151-152) and enrichFromOpenRouterSnapshot (line 202-203), the code checks `m.contextLength > 0` to decide whether to use live data or fall back to static data. However, when the live API returns 0 (indicating unknown/unset), it should not overwrite valid static catalog values. The current logic preserves the 0 when it should use the static value. This is backwards logic: fresh 0 values should be overridden by static data, not the other way around. Compare lines 151-152: `m.contextLength > 0 ? m.contextLength : stat.contextLength` — this keeps the live 0 instead of using stat. Should be: `m.contextLength > 0 ? m.contextLength : (stat.contextLength || 0)`
fix: Reverse the ternary logic when merging: use live data only if it's > 0, otherwise prefer static. This ensures stale live API responses with 0 don't override authoritative static metadata.

title: Unhandled promise rejection on import failure
detail: The getEncoder() function at lines 17-22 chains a dynamic import without error handling. If gpt-tokenizer fails to load, the promise rejects. The fire-and-forget call on line 28 (void getEncoder().then(...)) will not catch this rejection, causing an unhandled promise rejection that could terminate the process in strict environments or appear as a silent error.
fix: Add a .catch() handler to line 28 to handle import failures gracefully: void getEncoder().then((fn) => { encoderSync = fn; }).catch(() => { /* fallback to length/4 estimate */ });

title: Unsafe non-null assertion after prior null check
detail: Line 88 checks `if (!this.process?.stdin)`, but line 109 uses `this.process!.stdin!.write()` with force non-null assertions. Between lines 88 and 109, the process could theoretically exit and be cleared (though the timer is set up first). More critically, the check on line 88 guards only the immediate path; if an exception occurs between 88-109 (e.g., during Promise construction), the assertions are unsafe. Better to assign to a local variable after the check.
fix: Store process.stdin in a local variable after the check: `const stdin = this.process.stdin; if (!stdin) throw ...; return new Promise(...) { stdin.write(...) }`

title: Incorrect path handling with simple string replace
detail: The line `this.rootUri.replace('file://', '')` only removes the first occurrence of 'file://' and doesn't properly convert a file URL to a platform-specific path. On Windows, 'file:///C:/Users/...' becomes '/C:/Users/...' which is invalid. The correct approach is to use `fileURLToPath()` from 'node:url' which is already imported.
fix: Replace with `fileURLToPath(this.rootUri)`

title: Command injection via unescaped shell interpolation
detail: The execSync call interpolates the `command` variable directly into a shell command string: `execSync(`which ${command} 2>/dev/null || where ${command} 2>nul`, ...)`. While the current usage is from hardcoded BUILTIN_LSP_SERVERS array (low practical risk), this pattern is inherently unsafe and violates secure coding practices. A command with special characters (e.g., '`', '$', ';') could execute arbitrary shell code.
fix: Use the array form: `execSync(['which', command], {...})` or shell-escape the command parameter using a library like `shell-escape`.

title: Orphaned temporary file when renameSync fails
detail: In appendOnboardingResult, when writeFileSync(tmp, ...) succeeds but renameSync(tmp, file) fails, the catch block writes directly to the original file but never removes the temporary file at `${file}.${process.pid}.tmp`. This leaves orphaned temp files on disk.
fix: Add `unlinkSync(tmp)` in the catch block after the fallback write, or use a try-finally to ensure cleanup: `finally { try { unlinkSync(tmp); } catch {} }`

title: resolveApiKey bypasses sanitization unlike detectEnvKeys
detail: Line 309 calls `.trim()` on raw env var values. However, the code comment at lines 228-231 explicitly states sanitization should happen 'once at the boundary so downstream code never sees a dirty value.' The detectEnvKeys function (line 232) uses sanitizeApiKey which handles CRLF, BOM, control chars, etc., but resolveApiKey only trims. This inconsistency could allow unsanitized keys with hidden characters through.
fix: Replace `process.env[p.envKey]?.trim()` with `sanitizeApiKey(process.env[p.envKey] || '').value` to match detectEnvKeys' approach.

title: Unsafe type cast of unparsed JSON without validation
detail: Line 32 casts `parsed` to `Record<string, unknown>` without checking that it is actually a plain object. If the referenced JSON file contains a non-object value (array, string, number, boolean, null), the function would incorrectly treat it as a server map. For example, a JSON file containing `["server1", "server2"]` or `"invalid"` would pass through without error. The condition at line 29 only returns early if the object HAS the 'mcpServers' property, but if it lacks that property (including non-objects), line 32's cast is unsafe.
fix: Add a validation check before line 32: `if (!parsed || typeof parsed !== 'object' || Array.isArray(parsed)) { throw new PluginInstallError(...); }` to ensure parsed is a plain object before casting.

title: Orphaned permission rule for removed AgentStatus tool
detail: Line 88 includes a permission rule for AgentStatus: `{ tool: "AgentStatus", decision: "allow" }`, but AgentStatus is not included in GENERAL_BUILTIN_TOOLS (lines 34-67). The comment on lines 47-49 states that AgentStatus has been removed, but it's still in the permission rules. However, AgentStatus is still defined in the builtin tools registry (packages/core/src/tool-system/builtin/index.ts lines 192-199), creating an inconsistency: the permission rule references a tool that won't be available via the preset, but the tool still exists. This creates confusion about whether the tool is enabled or not.
fix: Remove line 88 `{ tool: "AgentStatus", decision: "allow" }` from GENERAL_PERMISSION_RULES to match the comment's assertion that AgentStatus has been removed from the preset toolset. If AgentStatus should remain available, add it to GENERAL_BUILTIN_TOOLS instead.

title: Redundant tool disable list passed to RunManager
detail: Lines 92-94 already filter enabledSet by removing tools from adapter?.disableTools. Then line 156 passes the filtered enabledSet as enabledBuiltinTools, AND line 157 passes the original adapter?.disableTools as disabledBuiltinTools. This double-application could cause RunManager to apply disable rules twice or create conflicting instructions about which tools are enabled.
fix: Either pass only the filtered enabledSet (remove line 157), or pass the original baseTools with disabledBuiltinTools list and let RunManager handle the filtering once. Choose one approach consistently.

title: Ambiguous system prompt precedence not enforced
detail: Lines 158-159 pass both customSystemPrompt (from presetDef.customPrompt) and appendSystemPrompt to RunManager. The ProductPreset interface allows both customPrompt and sections to be defined. The comment at lines 38-41 of types.ts documents that customPrompt should take precedence, but this function does not validate or enforce that — it could pass both sections and customPrompt to RunManager, causing ambiguity about which takes effect.
fix: Add validation: if presetDef.customPrompt is defined, do not build agentPreset.promptSections (set to empty array or undefined). Only pass one system prompt approach to RunManager.

title: System prompt uses UTC date instead of local date
detail: Line 64 uses `new Date().toISOString().split('T')[0]` which returns the UTC date, not the local date. For a user in UTC+8 on 2026-05-30 at 23:00, this returns 2026-05-29 (the next UTC day), not the local 2026-05-30. The system prompt should reflect the user's local date.
fix: Use `new Date().toLocaleDateString('en-CA')` instead of `new Date().toISOString().split('T')[0]` to get the local date in YYYY-MM-DD format.

title: Unsafe untyped property access on session objects
detail: Lines 549-555 access `(this.chatManager as any).sessions` and iterate over session objects, then call `s.isBusy()`, `s.queueDepth()`, and `s.lastActivityAt` without type safety. If the session object shape changes (e.g., method renamed, property removed), this fails silently at runtime. The code casts to `any` multiple times, bypassing TypeScript's type checking.
fix: Define a proper interface for session objects that chatManager.sessions contains, and either access the sessions through a typed getter method or assert the type properly instead of using `as any`.

title: isResponse type guard doesn't validate required fields
detail: The isResponse function at lines 326-328 only checks that 'method' is absent and 'id' is present. According to JSON-RPC 2.0 spec, a Response MUST have either a 'result' or 'error' field. The current check `"id" in msg && !("method" in msg)` allows invalid messages (with id but neither result nor error) to pass as valid responses.
fix: Add a check to verify at least one of 'result' or 'error' exists: `return "id" in msg && !("method" in msg) && ("result" in msg || "error" in msg);`

title: Missing write error handling in send()
detail: The write() call at line 70 does not handle backpressure or write errors. If the SSH stdin stream is closed or errors, the write silently fails (returns false on backpressure, throws on error). The caller has no way to know the message wasn't sent.
fix: Check the return value of write() and add an error handler to ssh.stdin, or use a Promise-based approach: return new Promise((resolve, reject) => { this.ssh.stdin.write(..., (err) => err ? reject(err) : resolve()); })

title: Incomplete resource cleanup: listeners not removed
detail: The disconnect() method kills the SSH process and clears the reference, but does not remove event listeners attached in connect() (lines 42, 48, 53). These listeners will remain in memory if the object is reused, causing memory leaks and potentially triggering handlers from previous connections.
fix: Store listener references and remove them: this.ssh.stdout?.off('data', handler); this.ssh.off('error', errorHandler); this.ssh.off('exit', exitHandler) before kill()

title: Command injection risk via identityFile and remoteCommand
detail: At lines 109 and 30, identityFile and remoteCommand from config are passed directly to spawn(). While spawn() doesn't use a shell by default (safer than exec), the remoteCommand is still concatenated as a shell argument. If remoteCommand comes from untrusted input, an attacker could inject shell metacharacters. For identityFile, path traversal is possible if not validated.
fix: Validate identityFile path (reject absolute paths, '..' sequences). For remoteCommand, avoid passing it as a single string; instead parse it into argv and pass as separate arguments, or use spawn('ssh', [...sshArgs, '--', remoteCmd]) with proper quoting

title: Incomplete path traversal validation
detail: The check `if (filePath.includes("..")) return;` only blocks directory traversal via `..` but does not prevent absolute paths (e.g., `/etc/passwd`), tilde-expansion (`~/sensitive`), or symlink attacks. An attacker or buggy caller could record artifacts outside the intended directory.
fix: Normalize the path and validate it is within the run's artifact directory using `path.resolve()` and checking it starts with the base directory, or reject absolute paths and paths containing `~`.

title: Regex for bash redirection doesn't handle quoted paths
detail: The pattern `/>\s*(\S+)\s*$/` matches the first non-whitespace sequence after `>` but fails on quoted paths with spaces: `cmd > "my file.txt"` will extract `"my` instead of the full path. This leads to incorrect artifact recording.
fix: Enhance the regex to handle both quoted and unquoted paths: `/[>|]\s*(['"]?)([^'"]*?)\1\s*$/` or use a proper shell parser.

title: Atomic write doesn't clean up temp file on failure
detail: If `renameSync(tmp, filePath)` throws an exception (e.g., permission denied, disk full), the temporary file is left behind at `filePath + ".tmp"` without cleanup. Over many failures, this accumulates orphaned files.
fix: Wrap the rename in a try-catch and delete the temp file on failure: `try { renameSync(tmp, filePath); } catch (e) { unlinkSync(tmp); throw; }`

title: Promise rejection in appendJsonl lock chain not handled
detail: If `appendFileSync()` throws inside the `.then()` callback (line 77), the returned `current` promise rejects. The lock map stores this rejected promise, and the next write will await the rejected promise, causing it to fail immediately without retrying. This breaks the serialize-writes guarantee.
fix: Catch and handle errors within the promise: `const current = prev.then(() => { try { appendFileSync(...); } catch (e) { console.error(...); throw; } }).catch(e => { this.appendLocks.delete(filePath); throw e; });` or use Promise.allSettled.

title: Missing timer cleanup in resolveApproval
detail: resolveApproval() does not clear the setTimeout that was created in requestApproval(). This means the timeout handler may still fire after the approval is resolved, potentially trying to resolve an already-resolved promise.
fix: Pass the timer reference back from requestApproval() or store it as instance state so resolveApproval() can clear it when called.

title: O(n) lookup in enqueue using array.includes()
detail: enqueue() uses this.pending.includes(runId) which is O(n) linear scan. For a queue with many runs, this becomes a performance bottleneck. Also checks this.active.has(runId) with a Set, creating inconsistency.
fix: Use a Set to track pending runIds instead of an array, changing pending to a Map or dual Set tracking for O(1) lookup.

title: Missing direct transitions from waiting/blocked states to running
detail: States waiting_input (line 207), waiting_approval (line 208), and blocked (line 209) can only transition to queued or cancelled, with no direct path to running. When user input is provided or approval is resolved, the run must go back to queued then to running, requiring two state transitions. If the intent is immediate resumption without re-queueing, these transitions should include 'running' as a valid next state.
fix: Add 'running' as a valid next state for waiting_input, waiting_approval, and blocked if immediate resumption is intended: waiting_input: ['running', 'queued', 'cancelled'], etc. Otherwise, clarify that re-queueing is intentional.

title: Nested setTimeout for SIGKILL creates unreferenced timer that could outlive result
detail: Lines 213-215 and 223-225 create a setTimeout for SIGKILL without storing the returned ID. The `.unref()` call prevents the timer from keeping the event loop alive, but if the promise resolves first, these timers remain pending. While unlikely to cause issues in practice, the pattern is fragile. Compare to how the main timeout timer is stored and cleared at line 253 and 281.
fix: Store the SIGKILL timer IDs and clear them in the finish() callback, or accept the async fire-and-forget pattern but document why this is safe.

title: Memory extraction only checks user scope for duplication, ignoring dream scope
detail: Line 75 creates a MemoryManager with default 'user' scope, then line 76 calls buildExtractionPrompt with only user-scope existing memories. However, the extraction prompt (extract-memories.ts line 48) instructs the model to 'Do not duplicate existing memories' without qualification. This could result in memories extracted to user scope that duplicate entries already in dream scope. The model has no visibility into dream-scope entries when making deduplication decisions.
fix: Load both user and dream scopes before extraction: change line 75 to load both scopes and pass them to buildExtractionPrompt, or clarify in the extraction prompt that deduplication only applies to user-scope memories since extraction always writes to user scope.

title: Command injection vulnerability in openBrowser URL
detail: The URL is directly interpolated into shell commands (lines 41, 43, 44) without escaping. If the OAuth authUrl contains special shell characters or quotes, it could allow command injection. For example, a malicious redirectUri with backticks or $() could execute arbitrary commands.
fix: Escape the URL using shell escaping (e.g., use 'printf %q' or a library like 'shellEscape'), or better yet, use Node.js APIs like 'open' package that don't invoke the shell for URL opening.

title: Incorrect 'most recent first' sorting for session memories
detail: The listSessionMemories() function sorts filenames alphabetically then reverses to get 'most recent first' (lines 53-54). However, if sessionIds are not lexicographically ordered by recency (e.g., UUIDs or non-timestamp formats), the sort order will not reflect actual recency. The comment on line 46 promises 'most recent first' but the implementation doesn't guarantee this without a timestamp field.
fix: Parse the createdAt timestamp from the SessionMemoryEntry objects and sort by that, or ensure sessionIds encode timestamps in a lexicographically comparable format (e.g., ISO 8601 strings, Unix timestamps).

title: Race condition in atomic write with process.pid-based temp file naming
detail: The temporary file name uses `process.pid` and `Date.now()` for uniqueness: `${target}.${process.pid}.${Date.now()}.tmp`. If two processes attempt concurrent saveState() calls on the same session with millisecond-level timing, or if a PID is recycled and reused, this could cause collisions where the rename() operation succeeds but silently overwrites another process's write. In a single-user CLI this is low-risk, but it's a potential lost-update bug.
fix: Use a cryptographically unique suffix like `nanoid()` instead of `process.pid.Date.now()`, or use a more robust atomic write library. Alternatively, document that concurrent writes to the same session are unsupported.

title: Memoization key generation calls statSync on every invocation, negating cache benefit
detail: The resolver function `(cwd: string) => \`${cwd}\0${userHome()}\0${installedPluginsMtime()}\`` calls `installedPluginsMtime()` on every memoized function invocation (line 206). This function performs a synchronous filesystem stat operation (line 198: `statSync(p).mtimeMs.toString()`). Even on cache hits, the resolver must execute to compute the cache key, incurring synchronous I/O overhead on the hot path. The mtime should be computed once and reused, or the key generation should be lazily evaluated only when needed.
fix: Move `installedPluginsMtime()` call outside the resolver, compute it once at module init and update it only when invalidateSkillCache() is called. Alternatively, use a two-level memoization where the mtime is cached separately.

title: waitForScrollIdle incorrectly clears flag then returns resolved promise
detail: The function `waitForScrollIdle()` at lines 63-66 sets `_scrollDraining = false` immediately and returns `Promise.resolve()`, without actually waiting for scroll activity to idle. This contradicts the function's name and purpose: callers expecting this to wait until scroll is complete will continue immediately instead. The flag is cleared before any caller can check if they should continue waiting.
fix: Either implement proper wait logic (e.g., via an event or settimeout loop checking scroll state), or rename the function to clarify it's a no-op. If this is intentionally a stub, add a comment explaining why, similar to other stubs in the file.

title: Code duplication: cancel() duplicates markFinished() field updates
detail: The cancel() method (lines 141-155) duplicates the field-setting logic from markFinished() (lines 119-127). Both methods set status, finishedAt, and finishedFadeAt in identical ways. While cancel() has additional abort handling (lines 146-149) and a guard against non-running agents (line 144), the timestamp-setting code should be extracted to avoid duplication and reduce maintenance burden.
fix: Extract the field-setting logic into a helper method or refactor cancel() to call markFinished() after handling abort, ensuring the guard check is preserved.

title: Blank lines treated as context lines without marker verification
detail: When a blank line (length === 0) is encountered in the patch body, the code unconditionally treats it as a context line, adding it to both oldLines and newLines (lines 263-264). However, the patch format requires all lines to have a marker prefix (' ', '+', or '-'). A truly blank line with no marker is ambiguous — it could indicate a blank line in the file or could be a parsing error. The code should either require a space marker for blank context lines or treat unmarked blank lines as an error (consistent with the behavior at line 282).
fix: Require blank lines to have an explicit ' ' marker prefix, treating truly blank lines (no marker) as an error or end-of-chunk signal.

title: Unsafe error property access in catch block
detail: The catch block at line 496-499 uses `(err as Error).message` without verifying err is an Error object. If err is a non-Error value (string, null, undefined), accessing .message will return undefined, resulting in the string 'Arena error: undefined'. This masks the actual error.
fix: Use `const msg = err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err);` or `const msg = (err as any)?.message ?? String(err);` to safely extract the error message.

title: Silent data loss when dot-notation key overwrites scalar values
detail: When setting a nested key like 'a.b.c' on settings containing {a: 'value'}, the code overwrites the scalar 'value' with an empty object {} at line 64. This silently loses data. For example, if settings.model = 'claude-opus', calling write with key='model.temperature' will delete the 'model' string and create an object instead.
fix: Check if the existing value is a non-object and either error with 'Cannot traverse non-object at {path}' or warn the user that existing data will be overwritten.

title: isNoMatchExit may mask real errors by over-generalizing exit code 1
detail: The function isNoMatchExit (line 29) returns true for any error with code 1 and empty stdout, but both ripgrep and grep exit with code 1 for multiple failure modes (permission denied, invalid regex, filesystem errors). Fallback logic at lines 85-94 treats this as 'no matches' and continues to try grep, which may then also fail. Real errors get masked by 'No matches found.' error message. Cite: line 31 condition `e?.code === 1 && (!e.stdout || e.stdout.trim() === "")`
fix: Distinguish between genuine 'no matches' (code 1 with empty output and no stderr) vs other failures. Either inspect stderr, or require a minimum message signature that is specific to 'no matches' error output from each tool.

title: Hardcoded 2-second timeout for LSP diagnostics is a race condition
detail: The getDiagnostics action waits exactly 2 seconds with `await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 2000))` for diagnostic results after sending didOpen notification. This is an arbitrary timeout that may be too short for some language servers or too long for others, causing intermittent failures or unnecessary delays. Diagnostics are typically delivered via LSP notifications, not request-response.
fix: Either: (1) Subscribe to LSP diagnostic notifications and collect results until no new ones arrive, or (2) accept timeout as a parameter, or (3) return immediately with a message that diagnostics will appear in LSP notifications.

title: Abort signal listener never removed on normal completion
detail: The event listener added at line 33 via `signal?.addEventListener("abort", ...)` is never removed when the promise resolves normally (timeout completes). This leaves a dangling listener on the AbortSignal for the lifetime of the signal object. While the signal itself is typically short-lived (tied to the tool invocation), this is a resource leak pattern that violates cleanup best practices.
fix: Use `signal?.addEventListener("abort", ..., { once: true })` to automatically remove the listener after it fires, or manually remove it: `const handler = () => {...}; signal?.addEventListener("abort", handler); ... signal?.removeEventListener("abort", handler);`

title: Full response loaded into memory before truncation
detail: At line 267, `await res.text()` loads the entire response body into memory before truncation checks at line 276. If a large file (e.g., 1GB) is fetched with `maxLength=50000`, the entire file is loaded first, then truncated. This can cause memory exhaustion. The 30-second timeout at line 214 mitigates some impact, but does not prevent large transfers from consuming excessive memory before truncation.
fix: For text responses, stream the response and truncate during reading, or limit the read size: `const reader = res.body.getReader(); const chunks = []; let size = 0; while (size < maxLength * 2) { const {done, value} = await reader.read(); ... }`

title: Incomplete escape sequence handling in SBPL profile path quoting
detail: The quote() function at lines 115-116 only escapes double quotes but not backslashes. In SBPL quoted string syntax, backslashes are escape characters. If a path contains a literal backslash (valid on Unix), it will not be properly escaped, causing potential sandbox profile mismatches. For example, a path like "/tmp\test" would generate (subpath "/tmp\test") which SBPL may parse incorrectly. This could cause the sandbox to fail to apply the intended restrictions or permissions for such paths.
fix: Escape backslashes before double quotes: return `"${path.replace(/\\/g, '\\\\').replace(/"/g, '\\"')}"`;  This ensures both backslashes and quotes are properly escaped in the SBPL string.

title: File descriptor not explicitly closed
detail: At line 333, a file descriptor is opened with fs.openSync() and passed to spawn() for stdio redirection. The file descriptor is never explicitly closed by the parent process. Although it's passed to the child process and the parent is exiting anyway (via process.exit handler), this represents a file handle that lingers until the parent process terminates. While not a critical leak since it's in an exit handler, it's not clean resource management.
fix: After spawn() returns, explicitly close the file descriptor with fs.closeSync(logFd), since the child process has inherited it and won't interfere with closure.

title: formatDuration incorrectly returns '0s' for milliseconds in range [1, 1000)
detail: The function checks 'if (ms < 60000)' then 'if (ms < 1)' for fractional ms, but the else clause at line 48 uses Math.floor(ms/1000) for all other values. For ms values from 1 to 999 (e.g., 500ms), this returns Math.floor(500/1000)=0, resulting in '0s' output. This is incorrect — 500ms should display as approximately '0.5s', not '0s'. The condition at line 44 only catches ms < 1 (less than 1 millisecond), leaving values 1-999ms to be floored to 0.
fix: After line 40, before line 48, add a check: if the floored value is 0 and ms > 0, format with decimals instead: const s = (ms / 1000).toFixed(1); return `${s}s`

title: Incorrect xterm-256 grayscale index formula
detail: The grayscale ramp mapping formula uses divisors 247 and multiplier 24, but should use 240 and 23. The xterm-256 grayscale ramp spans indices 232-255 (24 steps), mapping to RGB range [8,248] (240 units). The correct mapping is (r-8)/240*23, not (r-8)/247*24. This produces off-by-one index errors at certain RGB values (e.g., r=148 and r=168 map to 246 and 249 instead of 245 and 247).
fix: Change line 649 from `Math.round((r - 8) / 247 * 24) + 232` to `Math.round((r - 8) / 240 * 23) + 232` to correctly distribute 24 grayscale levels across the RGB range.

title: Line filtering loses intentional empty lines in log output
detail: Line 36: `raw.split("\n").filter(Boolean)` removes all empty lines including intentional ones. If a log message contains an empty line, subsequent entries are dropped. While unlikely, structured log lines or multi-line stack traces could be affected.
fix: Remove `.filter(Boolean)` and instead handle trailing newlines explicitly: `raw.split("\n").filter((l, i, arr) => i < arr.length - 1 || l.length > 0)` or just split and slice without filtering.

title: Repeated loadAll() calls for same memory manager state
detail: Both listMemory (line 51) and readMemory (line 68) independently call `mm(level, scope, cwd).loadAll()` without any caching. If the renderer calls both functions in sequence, the filesystem is read twice for the same data. The MemoryManager instance is created fresh each time.
fix: Cache the entries from loadAll() for the same (level, scope, cwd) tuple, or refactor to accept pre-loaded entries as an optional parameter.

title: Incomplete path traversal protection using string replacement instead of path.basename()
detail: Line 108 uses `runId.replace(/[\\/]/g, "")` to sanitize the runId before using it in path operations. While this removes forward and back slashes, it's a fragile approach that relies on removing path separators. A more robust solution would use `path.basename(runId)` which is explicitly designed for this use case and is the standard defensive practice.
fix: Replace line 108 with `const clean = path.basename(runId);` to use the standard library function for extracting the final path component, which is more explicit about intent and less error-prone.

title: Timers not stored or cleaned up on app shutdown
detail: Lines 101-102 create a setTimeout and setInterval that are never stored or cleared. While the `configured` flag prevents multiple timer allocations, the timers are not cleaned up when the app shuts down, potentially leaking memory. They should be stored and cleared in a cleanup handler.
fix: Store the timeout and interval IDs in module-scope variables, and export a cleanup function to clear them (or use a process.on('exit') handler). For example: `const timerId = setTimeout(...); const intervalId = setInterval(...);` and then in a cleanup function: `clearTimeout(timerId); clearInterval(intervalId);`

title: Stored window state values not validated, allowing invalid data to be used
detail: Line 19 casts JSON.parse result to Partial<WindowState> without type validation. If the stored JSON contains invalid values (e.g., width: "abc" or x: NaN), they bypass TypeScript's type checking and are spread directly into the return object. When this state is passed to BrowserWindow constructor (lines 108-111 in index.ts), invalid dimensions will cause undefined behavior.
fix: Add runtime validation after parsing JSON: check that width/height are positive numbers, x/y are numbers or undefined, and maximized is boolean or undefined. Return DEFAULT if validation fails.

title: msg.id type cast without validation; wrong id type causes silent loss
detail: Line 39 casts `msg.id as number` without validating it's actually a number. If a malformed message has `msg.id` as string or other type, the pending map lookup (line 40) silently fails and the response is discarded. The code should validate `typeof msg.id === 'number'` before treating it as a number.
fix: Add type check: `if (typeof msg.id !== 'number') return;` before line 40.

title: agent/approvalRequest passes undefined params to listeners
detail: Line 59 forwards `params` directly to `approvalListeners`, but `params` can be undefined (line 49). The handlers receive `undefined` instead of a well-formed approval request object. The method documentation suggests listeners expect `{ sessionId, requestId, request }` envelope but that structure is not validated.
fix: Add a null check and ensure params has the expected structure before forwarding: `if (params && params.sessionId && params.requestId) { approvalListeners.forEach(...) }`

title: Log function sends unsanitized user data to main process
detail: The `log()` API accepts `data?: Record<string, unknown>` and sends it directly via `ipcRenderer.send('desktop:log')` without validation or sanitization. If sensitive data (API keys, credentials, PII) is logged, it will be written to `~/.code-shell/logs/desktop-*.log` unfiltered.
fix: Add a sanitization step before sending: strip known sensitive keys (passwords, tokens, keys, secrets) or use a whitelist of allowed data keys.

title: Unsanitized URL passed to main process GitHub inspection handler
detail: `inspectGithubSkill(url: string)` accepts any URL string without validation and forwards it to `ipcRenderer.invoke('skills:inspectGithub')`. The main process likely fetches from this URL, making it vulnerable to SSRF attacks (e.g., attacker provides internal IP or file:// URL).
fix: Validate the URL: check it's a valid GitHub URL (hostname is github.com), reject non-HTTPS schemes, and reject internal IP ranges.

title: Stale closure in setApproval callback uses outer approvalQueue state
detail: In decideEnvelope(), line 745 removes the approval from approvalQueue via setApprovalQueue, but line 750 in the setApproval callback closes over the OUTER approvalQueue which still contains the removed item. Line 752 searches this stale queue for the next approval, potentially finding one that was already dequeued. The setApprovalQueue and setApproval updates are not synchronized.
fix: Use the filtered queue from setApprovalQueue callback, or structure the logic to derive next approval from the updated queue: setApprovalQueue((q) => { const filtered = q.filter((e) => e.requestId !== env.requestId); setApproval((cur) => filtered[0] ?? null); return filtered; });

title: IntersectionObserver created and attached to element that may change references
detail: The IntersectionObserver is set up with a dependency on `pendingApproval?.requestId`. When the requestId changes, the effect re-runs and a new observer is created. However, the ref `inlineApprovalRef` now points to a new DOM element, and if the previous observer was attached to an old element, it may not be properly cleaned up, potentially leaving observers dangling. Additionally, querying `el.closest(".stream")` on line 309 is re-executed on each effect run, which could return different results if the DOM structure changes.
fix: Ensure the observer is cleaned up before attaching to a new element. Consider using the full `pendingApproval` object (including envelope id) as a dependency, and validate that the old observer is disconnected before creating a new one.

title: Uncleanable timer may cause memory leak warning on unmount
detail: The onCopy callback sets a timer via window.setTimeout at line 112 that calls setCopied(false) after 1.5 seconds. If the component unmounts before the timer fires, React will warn about setting state on an unmounted component. The timer is not aborted on cleanup, making it impossible to prevent the state update.
fix: Use useEffect with a cleanup that aborts the timer, or store the timeout ID in a useRef and clear it on unmount. Example: store timeoutId in useRef, clear it on unmount or before setting new one.

title: Div with onFocus/onBlur handlers is not keyboard-focusable
detail: The div element at lines 98-129 has onFocus and onBlur event handlers (lines 105-106) but divs are not focusable by default in HTML. This means keyboard users cannot trigger the status popover via focus, only mouse users can via onMouseEnter. The div needs tabIndex={0} or similar to be focusable.
fix: Add tabIndex={0} to the div at line 98 to make it keyboard-focusable so focus/blur handlers can work.

title: Comment-code mismatch: filteredSkills limited to 8 not 6
detail: Line 75 documents 'Limit to the first 6 so long lists don't push files off-screen' but line 85 uses `.slice(0, 8)` instead of `.slice(0, 6)`. The implementation doesn't match the documented intent and design constraint.
fix: Change line 85 to `return matches.slice(0, 6);` to match the documented limit of 6 items.

title: Mutable Set used as React state initializer
detail: Line 27 initializes state with `useState<Set<number>>(() => new Set())`. While the initializer function pattern is correct, using mutable Set instances as state is problematic in React. Sets don't implement value equality, so if re-renders occur, the same Set reference from a previous render could cause unexpected behavior. The picked indices should be stored as an immutable structure (e.g., Set is fine for local mutations within setState, but the initialization pattern suggests this may be a source of bugs in parent/sibling updates).
fix: Consider using an immutable structure like `new Set<number>()` initialized inline, but more importantly, verify that Set comparisons work correctly across re-renders. If issues arise with stale Set references, convert to an array-based state: `useState<number[]>(() => [])` and use indexOf/filter for membership tests.

title: Uncleaned setTimeout in onUndoConfirmed creates memory leak
detail: The setTimeout() call on line 68 is never cleaned up. If the component unmounts or the modal closes before the 4-second timeout fires, the callback will still attempt to call setUndoStatus() on an unmounted component. While React will warn about this in development, the proper pattern is to wrap this in a useEffect with a cleanup function that clears the timeout.
fix: Wrap the setTimeout logic in a useEffect with proper cleanup: `useEffect(() => { const timeout = window.setTimeout(() => setUndoStatus(null), 4000); return () => clearTimeout(timeout); }, [undoStatus])` or alternatively store the timeout ID and clear it on unmount.

title: countLines() counts trailing newline as extra line, inconsistent with linesOf()
detail:
The countLines() function splits text by '
' without removing trailing newlines first, unlike linesOf() at line 107 which does: `text.endsWith("
") ? text.slice(0, -1).split("
") : text.split("
")`. This causes text ending with '
' to be counted 1 line too high. For example, "line1
line2
" would count 3 lines but linesOf() returns 2 lines. This discrepancy affects the added/removed line counts shown in the UI (lines 62-63, 74-75, 83-84, 93-94) which will not match the actual lines in the generated diffs.
fix:
Make countLines() consistent with linesOf(): `return typeof s === "string" && s.length > 0 ? (s.endsWith("
") ? s.slice(0, -1).split("
").length : s.split("
").length) : 0;`

title: Missing error handling in commitEdit allows silent failures
detail: The commitEdit function awaits window.codeshell.renameSession(editing, editDraft.trim()) at line 51 without a try-catch block. If the rename fails, the error is silently ignored but the UI state (setEditing(null) and setEditDraft("")) is still cleared at lines 52-53, leaving the UI in an inconsistent state where the user doesn't know the operation failed.
fix: Wrap the renameSession call in a try-catch block and only clear the editing state on success. Show an error message on failure.

title: Unhandled promise rejection in delete button handler
detail: The onClick handler at line 124-127 awaits window.codeshell.deleteSession(s.id) without a try-catch block. If deletion fails, the error is silently ignored but refresh() is still called at line 126, potentially showing stale UI state. Users won't know if deletion succeeded or failed.
fix: Wrap the deleteSession call in a try-catch block. Only call refresh() on success, and display an error message on failure.

title: Unhandled promise rejection in save function
detail: The save function awaits window.codeshell.saveAgent(draft) at line 90 without a try-catch for the success path. If the subsequent load() call at line 91 fails, the error won't propagate to the catch block and the UI will remain stale. Additionally, if saveAgent succeeds but load() fails, the user won't see the error.
fix: Add try-catch around both saveAgent and load calls. Ensure both operations succeed before updating selected state. Display error messages for each operation separately.

title: Missing error handling in remove function
detail: The remove function calls window.codeshell.deleteAgent(a.name) at line 106 without a try-catch block. If deletion fails, the error is silently ignored but UI state is still cleared at lines 107-109 (load() is called, then selected and draft are set to null), leaving the UI inconsistent.
fix: Wrap the deleteAgent call and subsequent load() call in a try-catch block. Only clear UI state on success. Display error messages to the user on failure.

title: HTTP headers formatted as KEY=VALUE instead of Key: Value
detail: Line 414 calls `envOrHeadersToText(initial?.headers)` which formats all key-value pairs as `KEY=VALUE` (line 646). However, HTTP headers should use `Key: Value` format as shown in the textarea placeholder at line 553. This causes headers to be displayed in the wrong format to the user, potentially confusing them or causing issues when they try to re-edit.
fix: Modify `envOrHeadersToText` to accept a format parameter, or create a separate function for headers. Alternatively, in the editor, use different formatting for headers: split on `:` and rejoin with `:` during round-trip.

title: canFetch condition incorrectly exempts openrouter from API key requirement
detail: Line 476 has condition: (!kindMeta.needsKey || Boolean(effectiveApiKey) || form.kind === "openrouter"). This exempts openrouter from requiring an API key, but KIND_META at line 82-87 shows openrouter has needsKey: true. This logic appears inverted - it should allow fetch without key only for providers where needsKey is false (like ollama at line 137-140), not for openrouter which requires a key.
fix: Remove the `|| form.kind === "openrouter"` clause. The condition should be: `Boolean(effectiveBaseUrl) && (!kindMeta.needsKey || Boolean(effectiveApiKey))`

title: No error handling for updateSettings failure
detail: The choose() function uses try-finally without a catch block. If window.codeshell.updateSettings() throws an error at line 42-48, the error is silently ignored and the user receives no feedback. The setSaving flag is reset to false but the error is never communicated to the user, unlike ModelSection.tsx which maintains and displays an error state.
fix: Add a catch block to handle and display errors: catch (e) { console.error(e); /* display error to user */ } or add error state management like ModelSection has.

title: Silent error handling in saveProvider with no user feedback
detail: The catch block in saveProvider (line 159) only resets saving: false but does not communicate the error to the user. If writeBack fails (e.g., due to permission issues or disk problems), the user sees the save button become enabled again but has no indication the save failed. The dirty flag is never reset on error, so users may think they need to resave or may not realize the operation failed.
fix: Add error state tracking to ProviderState (or panel-level) and display error messages to users. Consider setting dirty: true on error so the unsaved change is still visible, or show a toast/error message in the UI.

title: No error handling in clearProvider, risking UI/state desync
detail: clearProvider calls setByProvider(next) immediately (line 169) without awaiting or catching errors from writeBack (line 170). If writeBack fails, the UI will show the provider as cleared while the settings file still contains the old data. On next load, the data will reappear, confusing users.
fix: Wrap writeBack in try-catch and only call setByProvider after a successful write, or revert the state on error. Add error feedback to the user.

title: Cursor can become negative when ArrowDown pressed on empty list
detail: When `filtered.length === 0` and user presses ArrowDown, the cursor is set to `Math.min(0 + 1, 0 - 1)` which evaluates to `-1`. This violates the invariant that cursor should be in range [0, filtered.length - 1]. While the empty state prevents visual issues, it creates an invalid state. The same issue occurs at line 61 for ArrowUp when the cursor is already at 0 on an empty list (though that case is guarded by Math.max(0, ...)).
fix: Guard the cursor update to only apply when filtered.length > 0, or clamp the result: `setCursor((c) => filtered.length > 0 ? Math.min(c + 1, filtered.length - 1) : 0)`

title: Write success detection relies on fragile result string heuristic
detail: Lines 74-76 treat a Write/write_file call as successful if the result string doesn't start with 'error' (case-insensitive). This is insufficient: an empty result (line 75 coerces undefined to '') would pass the check and be marked successful even if the write failed. The logic doesn't validate that the result is actually present or meaningful, only that it doesn't start with 'error'. A result like 'Error: timeout' would correctly fail, but 'error' or '' would incorrectly succeed.
fix: Add an explicit check for truthy result before adding the attachment: `const lc = (result ?? "").toLowerCase(); if (lc && !lc.startsWith("error")) push(obj.file_path);` or use a more robust success indicator like checking for 'success' or 'wrote' keywords.

title: Unhandled promise rejection in getTrust call
detail: The promise returned by window.codeshell.getTrust(repoPath) at line 16 has no .catch() handler. If this promise rejects, it will cause an unhandled promise rejection, even though the cancelled flag check at line 17 prevents state updates. Rejections should be explicitly handled.
fix: Add .catch(err => { if (!cancelled) console.error(err); }) or use try-catch in an async IIFE

title: Missing try-catch on process.chdir()
detail: Line 65 calls process.chdir(cwd) without error handling. Although cwd is validated on line 38, the directory could become inaccessible between validation and the chdir call, or chdir could fail for other reasons (permissions, permission denied). This unhandled error would crash the setup process without a graceful error message.
fix: Wrap process.chdir(cwd) in a try-catch block with appropriate error messaging, similar to the validation on lines 36-44.

title: Missing clientDefaults in raw model path fallback
detail: In `resolveParticipants`, the raw model path fallback (lines 214-217) does not include `clientDefaults: { temperature: 0.3 }` that is present in other participant creation paths (lines 195, 210). This inconsistency may cause undefined behavior when using raw model paths.
fix: Add `clientDefaults: { temperature: 0.3 }` to the participant object created at lines 214-217, matching the pattern used elsewhere in the function.

title: Unnecessary use of shell command for file I/O
detail: Reading package.json uses `execSync(\"cat package.json\", ...)` instead of the safer `readFileSync(join(ctx.cwd, 'package.json'), 'utf-8')`. While ctx.cwd is not directly user-controlled here, using shell commands for file operations increases attack surface unnecessarily.
fix: Replace with `readFileSync(join(ctx.cwd, 'package.json'), 'utf-8')` from node:fs module for safer file I/O.

title: Silent error handling when parsing settings.json
detail: Empty catch block `} catch {}` silently ignores all JSON.parse errors when reading settings.json. If the file is corrupted or malformed, the error is suppressed and an empty object is used as fallback, potentially leading to loss of existing settings without any feedback to the user.
fix: Log the error or validate JSON parsing with explicit error handling, e.g., `} catch (err) { console.warn('Failed to parse settings:', err); }` to inform the user of potential data loss.

title: Synchronous execSync blocks event loop for clipboard operations
detail: Line 33 uses execSync which blocks the entire event loop for up to 5 seconds when copying to clipboard. This can freeze the UI. While the timeout mitigates runaway commands, the synchronous nature means any clipboard operation will block user interaction. Consider using async alternatives like execa or child_process.exec.
fix: Replace execSync with an async alternative: use `exec()` from child_process with a callback or promise wrapper to avoid blocking the event loop.

title: Missing await for async uninstallPluginByName
detail: Line 65 calls `uninstallPluginByName(name)` without await, but this appears to be called within an async function at line 75 that is itself awaited. If `uninstallPluginByName` is asynchronous, the promise is not being awaited, and the function may complete before uninstall finishes. The success message on line 67 could execute before the operation completes.
fix: Verify if uninstallPluginByName is async. If so, change line 66 to: `await uninstallPluginByName(name);`

title: Unclosed readline interface resource leak in start() method
detail: The readline interface created at line 31 (const rl = createInterface(...)) is never closed. When start() is called multiple times or in long-running processes, this leaks file descriptors. The interface should be closed with rl.close() after use, or the method should return the interface for caller management.
fix: Add rl.close() in the 'close' event handler, or return a cleanup function from start() that closes the readline interface when done.

title: Missing validation on parseInt for --limit option
detail: Line 91 calls `parseInt(opts.limit)` without validation. If the user passes a non-numeric value, `parseInt()` returns `NaN`, which is then passed to `sm.list(NaN)`, causing undefined behavior.
fix: Validate the parsed integer with Number.isNaN() or use parseInt with radix and validate the result before using it.

title: Missing validation on parseInt for --max-turns option
detail: Line 190 calls `parseInt(opts.maxTurns)` without validation. If opts.maxTurns is invalid, parseInt returns NaN, which is passed upstream without validation.
fix: Add validation with Number.isNaN() before returning, or use a safer parsing function.

title: JsonRenderer accumulates text across multiple calls without reset
detail: The JsonRenderer class stores accumulated text in `this.text` (line 92) but never resets it in onComplete(). If the same renderer instance is reused, text from previous invocations persists and contaminates subsequent results.
fix: Reset `this.text = ""` at the end of onComplete() or at the start of the next event cycle.

title: Unsafe type assertions bypass type safety for event properties
detail: Lines 25, 38, and 45 use `event as any` to access properties like `agentId`, `description`, and `error` that do not exist in the StreamEvent type definition. This bypasses TypeScript's type checking and could fail silently if the event structure changes.
fix: Define a proper discriminated union type for event subtypes or use type guards instead of `as any` assertions.

title: Comment claims UI components are exported, but they are not
detail: Lines 2-5 state: 'UI components exported for use from desktop or other consumers.' However, line 8 only exports VERSION from @cjhyy/code-shell-core. The ui/index.tsx file exports startInkRepl and InkReplOptions (public API functions), but these are not re-exported from the main index.ts entrypoint. Since package.json declares the main export as './dist/index.js' (built from this file) and there is no separate './ui' export path, external consumers expecting to import UI components from '@cjhyy/code-shell-tui' would only get VERSION.
fix: Either: (1) add 'export { startInkRepl, type InkReplOptions } from "./ui/index.js";' to match the comment's intent, or (2) update the comment to reflect that only VERSION is exported and consumers should import UI components via relative or sub-path imports.

title: No validation for negative count parameter
detail: Line 21 defaults count to 1 if undefined, but does not validate that count is a non-negative number. If a caller passes count={-1}, line 24 will throw a RangeError when calling String.prototype.repeat() with a negative argument. The JSDoc on line 5-8 states 'Number of newlines to insert' and line 14 says 'one or more', which implies count should be >= 1, but there's no runtime check.
fix: Add validation after line 21 to ensure count >= 1, e.g., const count = Math.max(1, t1 ?? 1);

title: Potential undefined access from memoizedStylesForWrap due to @ts-nocheck
detail: Line 242 accesses `memoizedStylesForWrap[wrap]` without validation. Due to `@ts-nocheck` on line 1, TypeScript's type safety is disabled. If an invalid `wrap` value is passed (not in the hardcoded keys on lines 61-110), this returns `undefined`, which is then passed to the JSX element on line 245. This could cause runtime errors or unexpected rendering behavior.
fix: Either remove `@ts-nocheck` to restore type safety, or add a runtime assertion/fallback: `const t15 = memoizedStylesForWrap[wrap] ?? memoizedStylesForWrap['wrap'];` to ensure a valid style object is always used.

title: WriteStream resource leak - stream never closed
detail: The WriteStream created at line 61 via `createWriteStream(join(dir, 'render-perf.log'), { flags: 'a' })` is stored in module-level variable `stream` and never explicitly closed. While Node.js will clean up handles on process exit, the file descriptor remains open for the lifetime of the process, and a proper close or end() should be called. This violates resource management best practices and could lead to file handle exhaustion if multiple instances are created.
fix: Add an explicit close mechanism - either store a cleanup function that calls `stream?.destroy()` and register it with `process.on('exit', ...)`, or call `stream.end()` when recording stops.

title: Stale visibility state returned when terminalSize context becomes undefined
detail: The useLayoutEffect (line 46) has no dependency array and runs every render. However, if terminalSize becomes undefined/falsy after previously being defined, the effect returns early (line 49) without updating entryRef. The hook then returns the stale entryRef.current (line 95) with the old visibility value. If terminalSize can toggle between defined and undefined states (e.g., during responsive terminal resizing), callers receive incorrect stale visibility data.
fix: Either add terminalSize to a dependency array (though this defeats the design of running every render), or reset entryRef.current to a safe default value when terminalSize is unavailable: `if (!element?.yogaNode || !terminalSize) { entryRef.current = { isVisible: true }; return }` (line 48-50).

title: Loop processes text one position past the end, creating spurious empty line
detail: The condition 'while (start <= text.length)' allows the loop to iterate when start equals text.length. On this final iteration, text.substring(start) returns empty string, which gets counted as a line (height++). For text ending with a newline like 'hello\n', this creates an extra empty line in the height count.
fix: Change loop condition to 'while (start < text.length)' or add explicit check to break when start === text.length after handling the newline. Verify expected behavior for trailing newlines matches the new implementation.

title: Unnecessary loop iteration at maxY boundary
detail: Line 369: The loop condition `for (let row = startY; row <= maxY; row++)` includes maxY, but the subsequent logic at line 379 treats it as a sentinel value only for flushing. When `row === maxY`, the condition `row < maxY` at line 371 is false, so `excluded` is always false, and we enter the flush block unnecessarily. While this doesn't cause incorrect output (the flush with `row === maxY` is a no-op when `row === rowStart`), it wastes a loop iteration.
fix: Change the loop condition to `row < maxY` and restructure to handle the final flush explicitly after the loop, or adjust the logic to avoid the redundant iteration.

title: O(n*m) loop checking absoluteClears for every row during blit
detail: Line 372: `absoluteClears.some(r => ...)` is called for every row in the blit region. With M rows and N absolute clears, this is O(M*N). For large screens with multiple absolute-positioned nodes (common in layouts with headers/sidebars/pills), this becomes a hot-path bottleneck executed every frame.
fix: Build a Set or Map of row ranges covered by absoluteClears during the initial collection (lines 286-303), or use a spatial index to quickly query which rows intersect with absolute clear regions.

title: splitNumericParams does not handle empty numeric parameters
detail: The function splits by ';' and parses each part with parseInt. If the input contains empty segments (e.g., '1;;3' from malformed DA1 responses), parseInt('', 10) returns NaN instead of a sensible default. This would create NaN entries in the params array, potentially breaking downstream code expecting only valid numbers.
fix: Filter out empty strings before parsing, or use parseInt with a fallback default: `return params.split(';').map(p => parseInt(p || '0', 10))`

title: Unvalidated URL in OSC 8 hyperlink sequences
detail: The wrapWithOsc8Link function does not validate or escape the url parameter before inserting it into an OSC 8 escape sequence. If url contains the bell character () or other escape sequences, it could break the hyperlink wrapper and malform the terminal output. An attacker controlling link text could inject terminal escape codes.
fix: Validate and escape the URL before use. At minimum, reject URLs containing  (BEL) or  (ESC). Consider using encodeURI or similar to sanitize the URL.

title: Absolute-positioned element y-clamping not reflected in cached geometry
detail: When an absolute-positioned element computes y < 0, line 459 clamps it to 0 for rendering. However, the cached rect stored at line 546 and 1241 uses this clamped y value. On the next frame, the node's cached.y will be 0 (the clamped value), not the actual computed yogaTop. This causes the blit-check comparison at line 470-471 to incorrectly believe the position hasn't changed when it actually has, potentially skipping necessary re-renders or causing visual glitches with partially off-screen absolute overlays.
fix: Store the actual computed yogaTop separately from the rendered y position. Use the actual value for cache comparison checks, and only clamp y when writing to output.

title: Resource leak: createRoot() doesn't clean up instances map on unmount
detail: The createRoot() function registers the Ink instance in the instances map (line 150: instances.set(stdout, instance)) but the returned Root object's unmount() method (line 154-155) only calls instance.unmount(), never instances.delete(stdout). If a user creates multiple roots on the same stdout stream and unmounts them, getInstance() will return the previously unmounted instance instead of creating a fresh one. Compare to renderSync() which provides a cleanup() method (line 103) that properly deletes from the map.
fix: Return a Root object with an additional cleanup() method that calls instances.delete(stdout), or have unmount() automatically clean up the instance from the map.

title: Inconsistent word1 index calculation uses bitwise OR instead of addition
detail: Line 1294 uses `nextCells[ci | 1]` to compute the word1 index, while the entire codebase consistently uses `ci + 1` for this purpose (lines 604, 658, 826, 1220, 1423, etc.). Although bitwise OR produces the same result for even `ci`, this inconsistency suggests a copy-paste error and is fragile to future refactoring. The correct pattern is `nextCells[ci + 1]`.
fix: Change line 1294 from `if (nextCells[ci] === 0 && nextCells[ci | 1] === 0)` to `if (nextCells[ci] === 0 && nextCells[ci + 1] === 0)`

title: Dead code: resolvers set is never populated
detail: Lines 18-23 iterate over `resolvers` set and call resolve() on each item when focus is lost. However, the `resolvers` Set is declared at line 9 but never populated anywhere in this file. This means the loop executes over an empty set and has no effect. Either there should be code to add items to `resolvers` elsewhere, or this is incomplete/dead code.
fix: Either populate the `resolvers` set in `waitForBlur()` or similar function, or remove the dead code at lines 18-23 if this functionality is not needed.

title: Incomplete escape sequence handling at end of input
detail: The `splitTabStatusPairs()` generator doesn't validate the escape state at the end of parsing. If input ends with a backslash (e.g., `"status=test\\"`), the `esc` flag remains true but is not checked before yielding. Line 392 yields `[key, val]` without first checking if `esc` is true, potentially leaving the trailing escape character in the output instead of treating it as an incomplete escape sequence.
fix: Before the final yield on line 392, check if `esc` is true (indicating a trailing backslash), and either: (1) append the backslash to the key/val, or (2) treat it as a syntax error depending on the protocol spec.

title: Unsafe non-null assertion on optional parameter
detail: Line 59 uses `wrapType!.startsWith('truncate')` with a non-null assertion on the parameter. The parameter type `Styles['textWrap']` is optional (can be undefined). Although callers in render-node-to-output.ts use nullish coalescing (`node.style.textWrap ?? 'wrap'`) to ensure a value is passed, the function signature permits undefined. If undefined is passed, this will throw a TypeError. The parameter type should be narrowed to exclude undefined, or the non-null assertion should be guarded by a prior check.
fix: Either: (1) Add a check: `if (wrapType && wrapType.startsWith('truncate'))`, or (2) Change the parameter type to `Exclude<Styles['textWrap'], undefined>` to reflect that it's never actually undefined, and remove the non-null assertion.

title: Inconsistent indentation of clearThinkingBuffer() call
detail: Line 531 has 'clearThinkingBuffer()' indented at column 6 (6 spaces), while surrounding code at lines 529-530 (setStreamMode, setThinkingContent) is indented at column 10 (10 spaces). This is inconsistent indentation that may indicate a refactoring error, though functionally it still executes correctly as it's within the same case block.
fix: Align the indentation of line 531 to match lines 529-530 for consistency.

title: Inconsistent indentation of clearThinkingBuffer() call in Ctrl+C handler
detail: Line 1042 has 'clearThinkingBuffer()' indented at column 6 (6 spaces), while surrounding code at lines 1040-1041 (setStreamMode, setThinkingContent) is indented at column 8 (8 spaces). Similar inconsistent indentation pattern to line 531.
fix: Align the indentation of line 1042 to match lines 1040-1041 for consistency.

title: useEffect dependency array uses boolean comparison instead of actual value
detail: The dependency array is `[visible.length === 0]` which compares to a boolean primitive. Since `visible` is recomputed on every render (line 45), the dependency should be `[visible.length]` to track when the actual agent list size changes. Currently, the interval may not restart when agents appear/disappear, leading to stale state or missed updates.
fix: Change the dependency array from `[visible.length === 0]` to `[visible.length]` on line 67.

title: Inconsistent border width calculations in code block
detail: Line 28 creates a top border with `Math.max(0, 40 - language.length)` padding to aim for ~40 characters, but line 41 creates the bottom border with a hardcoded `44` repeat count. These don't align—the borders won't be the same width, especially when language names are longer. The top border is also too short to align with the bottom.
fix: Calculate a consistent border width. Use the same logic for both borders, or measure the actual content width and apply consistent padding. For example, both should use 44 dashes, or calculate based on actual terminal width.

title: Dead condition in handleSubmit logic
